coredata.dc:title: A simple carbon dots based method to discriminate doxycycline, chlortetracycline from tetracyclines
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jinping
Qu, Yujiao
Wang, Xiuxia
Pan, Wei
Sun, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: It is rather difficult to differentiate tetracyclines (TCs) by only using carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescence methods due to their similar structures and optical properties. In this work, a simple method based on CDs to discriminate doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC) from TCs by combining the unique optical properties of the CDs and the complex of DOX with Ca2+. The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid and ethylene diamine. DOX and Ca2+ can form a stable complex with weak fluorescence emission at 511 nm, which can be significantly enhanced by the CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), accompanied by a decrease in the fluorescence peak of the CDs at 390 nm due to inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, CTC emits very weak fluorescence at 428 nm, which can be greatly enhanced by the CDs due to FRET. Correspondingly, DOX and CTC can be discriminated from the mixture solution of DOX, CTC and tetracycline (TC). In addition, TC could quench the fluorescence of the CDs due to IFE. Therefore, the CDs also can be used as a probe to detect TC in the samples only containing itself without interferences from other TCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tetracycline antibiotics
Ratiometric method
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TCs in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
Discrimination of DOX, CTC from TCs
Quantitative detection methods for DOX, CTC and TC
Determination of TCs in actual samples containing only one of them
Discriminate and quantitatively detect DOX and CTC in real samples containing the mixture of DOX, CTC and TC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) Particle size distribution, (C) FT-IR spectra, and (D) XRD pattern of the CDs.

(A) The excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs. Effects of (C) potential interference; (D) pH value (Red line) and ionic strength (Blue line) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of DOX at the concentrations in the range from 2 to 10 μM. (B) Fluorescence spectra of DOX at the concentrations in the range from 2 to 10 μM in the presence of Ca2+. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of DOX at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 10 μM in the presence Ca2+; and the linear relationships between (F511/F390) and the concentrations of DOX (inset). (D) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of DOX at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 10 μM in the absence of Ca2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CTC at the concentrations in the range from 2 to 10 μM. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CTC at the concentrations in the range from 2 to 10 μM in the presence of Ca2+. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of CTC at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 8 μM; and the linear relationship between (F/F0) and the concentrations of CTC (inset). (D) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of CTC at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 10 μM in the presence Ca2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TC at the concentrations in the range from 2 to 50 μM. (B) Fluorescence spectra of TC at the concentration sin the range from 2 to 50 μM in the presence of Ca2+. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of TC at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 50 μM; and the linear relationship between (F/F0) and the concentration of TC (inset). (D) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of TC at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 50 μM in the presence of Ca2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs upon addition of DOX at the concentrations in the range from 0 to 10 μM in the presence of Ca2+. (B) the linear relationship between F/F0 at 511 nm and the concentrations of DOX.

Schematic illustration of detection mechanism of doxycycline, chlortetracycline and tetracycline based on CDs and discrimination of doxycycline, chlortetracycline from tetracyclines with assistance from Ca2+.
Analytical results for the detection of DOX in tap water and pork samples.
Analytical results for the detection of CTC in tap water and pork samples.
Analytical results for the detection of TC in tap water and pork samples.
Analytical results for the detection of DOX in tap water and pork samples mixed with CTC, TC (2.50:2.50:2.50 μM, 5.00:5.00:5.00 μM and 7.50:7.50:7.50 μM).
Analytical results for the detection of CTC in tap water and pork samples mixed with DOX, TC (2.50:2.50:2.50 μM, 5.00:5.00:5.00 μM and 7.50:7.50:7.50 μM).

head.ce:title: A simple carbon dots based method to discriminate doxycycline, chlortetracycline from tetracyclines

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is rather difficult to differentiate tetracyclines (TCs) by only using carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescence methods due to their similar structures and optical properties. In this work, a simple method based on CDs to discriminate doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC) from TCs by combining the unique optical properties of the CDs and the complex of DOX with Ca2+. The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid and ethylene diamine. DOX and Ca2+ can form a stable complex with weak fluorescence emission at 511 nm, which can be significantly enhanced by the CDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), accompanied by a decrease in the fluorescence peak of the CDs at 390 nm due to inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, CTC emits very weak fluorescence at 428 nm, which can be greatly enhanced by the CDs due to FRET. Correspondingly, DOX and CTC can be discriminated from the mixture solution of DOX, CTC and tetracycline (TC). In addition, TC could quench the fluorescence of the CDs due to IFE. Therefore, the CDs also can be used as a probe to detect TC in the samples only containing itself without interferences from other TCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), recently, have attracted much more interest in analytical area because of their high performances [1–4]. For example, CDs possess excellent optical properties including intensive and tunable emissions, which makes CDs be able to produce strong and multi-color response signals [5–7]. Moreover, facile preparation and modification enable CDs to meet the diversity of detection functions with effective cost [8–10]. In addition, excellent biocompatibility [11,12], low toxicity [13,14] and environment-friendliness [15,16] are the excellent qualities of CDs that are highly required in analysis, particularly in bioanalysis [11,12,17]. As such, CDs as probes have aroused increasing attention in the detections of veterinary drugs which are widely used to prevent and treat diseases, and to promote the growths of food animals [4,18–21]. Among the detection methods for veterinary drugs, most are observed for tetracyclines (TCs) which are a class of antibiotics [22–24], including tetracycline (TC) [25], chlortetracycline (CTC) [26], metacycline (MTC) [27] and doxycycline (DOX) [28,29]. However, the CDs based detection methods for TCs were developed mostly on the mechanism of inner filter effect (IFE) [25,30–32]. That is, there is a wide absorption band ranging from 200 to 400 nm with two peaks at 260 and 355 nm in the absorption spectra of TCs. When TCs meet the CDs with excitation or emission spectra locating at around 350 nm, IFE will occur, resulting in TCs concentration dependent fluorescence quenching of CDs.

However, due to their similar structures and optical properties, it is rather difficult to differentiate TCs by only using CDs based fluorescence methods with an IFE mechanism. To date, in combination of the unique properties of CDs with mathematic tools and metal ions, a few strategies have been developed to distinguish the specific types of TCs. For instance, linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and support vector machine (SVM) mathematic tools were applied to support a dual-channel fluorescence sensor array based on two kinds of CDs to accurately differentiate the four TCs (TC, oxytetracycline (OTC), MTC and DOX), in which, the two kinds of CDs displayed concentration-dependent-IFE based fluorescence responses to the four TCs while LDA and SVM analyzed and processed the multidimensional data to classify the four TCs [33]. In addition, Al (III) was applied to assist dual-emission CDs to differentiate TC from other TCs. The main mechanism of this method is that Al (III) could induce the enhancement of the yellow emission from the CDs with a blue shift, which was inhibited by TC forming a strong complex with Al (III) [34]. Moreover, Miao et al. tried to distinguish TC, CTC and OTC only by using one type of CDs synthesized with tobacco as a raw precursor in NaOH solution [35]. In this approach, the three TCs could induce different changes in fluorescence emission of the CDs. CTC induced the emission of the CDs a blue shift by enlarging its energy band gap, and OTC caused a red shift for the emission of the CDs by narrowing down its band gap. Accordingly, the discrimination of the three TCs was achieved on a test strip through different emission color of the CDs induced by them.

Considering that mathematic tools are complex and need professional training and it is of low probability to obtain CDs which can discriminate TCs on their own, in this work, a simple method based on CDs to discriminate DOX, CTC from TCs by combining the unique optical properties of the CDs and the complex of DOX with Ca2+ (Scheme 1 ). The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid and ethylene diamine. The as-obtained CDs can significantly enhance the weak fluorescence emission at 511 nm from the complex of DOX with Ca2+ through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the fluorescence peak at 390 nm of the CDs gradually decreased upon addition of DOX due to IFE. Moreover, the CD greatly enhanced the very weak fluorescence at 428 nm of CTC due to FRET. Correspondingly, DOX and CTC can be discriminated from the mixture solution of DOX, CTC and TC. In addition, TC could quench the fluorescence of the CDs due to IFE. Therefore, the CDs also can be used as a probe to detect TC in the samples only containing itself without interferences from other TCs.

Briefly, 0.1 g phthalic acid (PA) and 0.8 mL ethylene diamine (EDA) were dissolved in 25 mL ultrapure water, quickly transferred into a 50-mL Teflon autoclave at 200 °C, and heated for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperture, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles, and the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtered CDs solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 12 h and finally lyophilized into solid CDs.

300 μL CDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 150 μL Ca2+ (20 mM) were put into a 10 mL volumetric flask in turn. Subsequently, 1 mL of DOX with different concentrations was added into the previous solution, and then diluted to 10 mL with PB solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). After incubation for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra were scanned by F-7000 under excitation at 320 nm.

Similarly, 300 μL CDs solution (1 mg/mL), 1 mL TCs (TC or CTC) solution with different concentrations were added into a 10 mL volumetric flask, then diluted to 10 mL with PB solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). After incubation for 30 min, the fluorescence spectra were scanned by F-7000 under excitation at 320 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and pork were chosen as samples to detect the TCs to demonstrate the feasibility of the method. Tap water was taken from our laboratory and first filtered with qualitative filter paper, then pretreated with 0.22 μm filtration membrane to remove solid suspensions and other impurities. Fresh pork was bought from a local market and crushed (1 g), then it was placed in a mixture solution of acetonitrile / water (4: 1) and homogenized for 3 min. Subsequently, each sample was vortexed and sonicated for 10 min. The obtained solution was transferred to centrifuge tubes and centrifuged with 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble materials, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and 8 mL of the supernatant was diluted with ultrapure water to 200 mL. Different concentrations of DOX, CTC, TC solutions (25, 50 and 75 μM) were spiked into the tap water and pork extract. Finally, 300 μL CDs solution, 150 μL Ca2+ and 1 mL DOX solution were put into a 10 mL volumetric flask in turn, the final concentration of DOX were 2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 μΜ, respectively. The detections of CTC and TC were similar to DOX without Ca2+. After incubated for 30 min, the corresponding concentration of TCs can be determined by the F-7000. As for the measurement of mixed samples, 300 μL CDs, 150 μL Ca2+ and 1 mL solution of DOX, CTC and TC were added to the volumetric flask respectively, the final concentrations of DOX, CTC and TC were 2.5 μΜ: 2.5 μΜ: 2.5 μΜ, (5.0 μΜ: 5.0 μΜ: 5.0 μΜ and 7.5 μΜ: 7.5 μΜ: 7.5 μΜ). After incubated 30 min, the corresponding concentrations of TCs were determined by F-7000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid (0.1 g) and ethylene diamine (0.8 mL) at 200 °C for 8 h. The morphology and the particle size distributions of the CDs were characterized by TEM. As can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. 1A, the CDs are well distributed with a nearly spherical morphology and an average size of 2.6 ± 0.6 nm obtained by counting about 100 dots( Fig. 1B). Obviously, a significantly broad absorption band at 3000 cm−1 is observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs, belonging to the stretching vibration of OH in the dimer of carboxyl acid on its surface [36]. The characteristic absorption peaks of C=O at 1646 cm−1, NH2 at 1560 cm−1, C-N at 1380 cm−1, and C–O–C at 1035 cm−1 are exhibited in the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs [37–39].The peak at 1510 cm−1 is assigned to the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring [40]. Therefore, the FTIR results suggest that there are carboxyl acid, benzene ring, C–O–C and C–N groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 1C). XRD pattern was applied to characterize the crystalline of the CDs and depicted in Fig. 1D. There is a weak diffraction broad peak at 25.8° in the XRD pattern, corresponding to the (002) diffraction facets of graphitic carbons. And many sharp peaks existing in the XRD pattern are indicative of densely packed chains with multiple nitrogen- and oxygen-contained groups, and benzene rings from phenolphthalein and EDA on the surface of graphitic carbon core [41].

As shown in Fig. 2 A, there is a strong absorption band at 280 nm ascribed to π-π* transition of C=C, and a weak absorption peak at 320 nm assigned to n-π* transition of C=O in the absorption spectrum of the CDs. The excitation spectrum of the CDs peaks at 320 nm, suggesting that nonradiative transition scarcely occurred in the CDs [42,43]. From the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2B, an excitation-dependent emission property is observed for the CDs, that is, the emission peak is red shifted and the corresponding intensity increases firstly and then decreases as the excitation wavelength becomes longer. And the maximum emission is located at 390 nm under excitation at 320 nm with an absolute quantum yield of 12.23%.

As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence of the CDs was hardly affected by metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+ and Al3+ etc. 200 μM), anions (Cl−, NO3 – and NO2 –, 200 μM), some small biological molecules (AA, L-cysteine and glucose, 50 μM) and antibiotics (penicillin (PNC), streptomycin (SM), 50 μM). Obviously, it was quenched by TC, MTC, DOX and OTC (50 μM) and enhanced by CTC (10 μM). It can be found that the fluorescence of the CDs was weaker under acidic conditions than under alkaline conditions, meanwhile, it fluctuated slightly in the range of 3–7 and 8–12, respectively (Fig. 2D, red line). In addition, the fluorescence of the CDs showed negligible change as the concentration of NaCl increased from 0.0 to 1.0 M (Fig. 2D, blue line), suggesting that the ionic strength variation could exert little effect on the fluorescence of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of DOX, CTC from TCs
ce:section.ce:para: DOX itself can emit weak concentration-dependent fluorescence at 511 nm (Fig. 3 A). Although literatures reported that DOX and Ca2+ can form a stable complex with rigid structure [44,45], as can be seen from Fig. 3B, slightly stronger DOX-concentration-dependent fluorescence was generated from the complex of DOX with Ca2+. Interestingly, upon addition of DOX into the solution of the CDs in the presence of Ca2+, the fluorescence band at 511 nm was sharply enhanced, meanwhile, the fluorescence peak at 390 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 3C). As shown in Figure S1A, the absorption band at 356 nm of the Ca (II)-DOX complex is overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Therefore, the Ca(II)-DOX complex exerted IFE on the fluorescence of the CDs, which is the reason for the decrease in the fluorescence at 390 nm. And FRET from the CDs to Ca(II)-DOX resulted in the fluorescence enhancement at 511 nm. In order to confirm the role of Ca2+ in the enhancement of the fluorescence at 511 nm, various concentrations of DOX were added into the aqueous solution of the CDs in the absence of Ca2+. The obtained results in Fig. 3D show a decrease in the fluorescence peak at 390 nm and no fluorescence emission at 511 nm. Therefore, the CDs can enhance the fluorescence emission of the complex of DOX with Ca2+ is the characteristic optical property to discriminate DOX from TCs.

As shown in Figure S3, the concentration of the CDs exerted no effect on the detection of DOX because F/F0 showed a negligible fluctuation when the CDs with different concentrations from 10 to 50 μM were added into the solution of DOX (50 μM). Upon addition of Ca2+ into the mixture solution of the CDs (30 μM) and DOX (50 μM), the fluorescence intensity at 511 nm reached the maximum when the concentration of Ca2+ was 300 μM. Therefore, 300 μM was selected as the concentration of Ca2+ for the following detection of DOX.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, CTC can also generate weak concentration-dependent fluorescence at 428 nm. However, the addition of Ca2+ into the aqueous solution of CTC did not affect its fluorescence emission (Fig. 4B). Notably, upon addition of CTC into the aqueous solution of the CDs, the fluorescence emission gradually red shifted from 390 nm to 428 nm, and the intensity at 428 nm is considerably stronger than the sum of their individual fluorescence intensities of the CDs and CTC as shown in Fig. 4C. Therefore, the fluorescence enhancement should result from FRET from the CDs to CTC due to the absorption band of CTC at 380 nm is entirely overlapped with the emission spectrum of the CD (Figure S1A), rather than aggregation caused fluorescence enhancement (Figure S2). Furthermore, in order to check whether CTC can also form a complex with Ca2+ producing an emission at 511 nm, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution mixed with CTC at different concentrations in the presence of Ca2+ were collected and are shown in Fig. 4D. No fluorescence peak appears at 511 nm in the collected spectra. Therefore, CTC would not interfere the discrimination of DOX with assistance from Ca2+. And FRET from the CDs to CTC can induce the fluorescence emission of CTC at 428 nm to be considerably increased, which is the specific optical property to discriminate CTC from TCs.

As shown in Fig. 5 A, TC can also emit weak concentration-dependent fluorescence at 511 nm. As well, slightly stronger TC-concentration-dependent fluorescence was generated from the complex of TC with Ca2+ (see Fig. 5B). Obviously, upon addition of TC into the aqueous solution of the CDs, the fluorescence emission at 390 nm gradually decreased due to the IFE of TC on the fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. 5C). Shown in Fig. 5D is the fluorescence spectra of the CDs mixed with TC at different concentrations in the presence of Ca2+. And no fluorescence peak at 511 nm can be found in the spectra. Therefore, TC would not interfere the discrimination of DOX with assistance from Ca2+.

In summary, DOX and CTC can be differentiated from TCs by the following specific optical properties: the emission of DOX at 511 can be significantly enhanced by the CDs in the presence of Ca2+; FRET from the CDs to CTC can induce the fluorescence emission of CTC at 428 nm to be considerably increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection methods for DOX, CTC and TC
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3C, upon addition of DOX into the solution of the CDs in the presence of Ca2+, the fluorescence intensity at 511 nm was greatly enhanced, meanwhile, the fluorescence peak intensity at 390 nm gradually decreased. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent method could be developed for the quantitative detection of DOX using the CDs as a probe with assistance from Ca2+. The ratio of the intensity at 511 nm to that at 390 nm (F511/F390) was used as the response signal. Linear relationships were built between F511/F390 and the concentration of DOX in the two ranges of 0.2–2.0 μM and 2.0–10.0 μM. The regression equations are F511/F390 = 0.08501c + 0.07229 (R2 = 0.984) and F511/F390 = 0.03004c + 0.17885 (R2 = 0.971), respectively, with a limit of detection of 19.13 nM. As shown in Fig. 4C, upon addition of CTC into the aqueous solution of the CDs, the intensity of fluorescence emission at 428 nm gradually increased, which is sensitive to the concentration of CTC. As a result, the CDs can be used as probe to quantitative detection of CTC. A linear relationship could be obtained between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration in the range of 0.2–8.0 μM with the equation of F/F0 = 0.452c + 1.15715 (R2 = 0.997) and a LOD as low as 13.45 nM.

As shown in Fig. 5C, upon addition of TC into the aqueous solution of the CDs, the fluorescence emission at 390 nm gradually decreased due to the IFE, suggesting the fluorescence quenching is sensitive to the concentration of TC. The CDs can be used as a probe to quantitatively detect TC in aqueous solution. A linear equation, F/F0 = − 0.01073c + 0.97146 (R2 = 0.987), can be fitted between the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm and the concentration of TC in the range of 2.0–50.0 μM with a LOD of 0.94 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCs in actual samples containing only one of them
Discriminate and quantitatively detect DOX and CTC in real samples containing the mixture of DOX, CTC and TC

ce:section.ce:para: Although it is difficult to differentiate TCs due to their similar structures and optical properties, the CDs synthesized by a hydrothermal treatment of phthalic acid and ethylene diamine can be developed as a probe to discriminate DOX, CTC from TCs with assistance from Ca2+. It can be concluded that CDs can discriminate TCs by producing unique emissions at specific wavelengths through direct interaction between TCs and CDs or indirect interaction with assistance from other substances. For example, in this work, the CDs can significantly enhance the weak fluorescence emission of the complex of DOX with Ca2+ at 511 nm, and the direct interaction between CTC and the CDs can produce a very strong emission at 428 nm. Correspondingly, DOX and CTC can be discriminated from the mixture solution of DOX, CTC and TC. Generally, TC can quench the fluorescence of CDs due to IFE. Therefore, CDs can be used as a probe to detect TC in the samples only containing itself without interferences from DOX and CTC.

Jinping Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Yujiao Qu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Investigation. Xiuxia Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei Pan: Investigation. Xiaobo Sun: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0105200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109955.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
